infobox.key: [Principal symbol of Christianity](/wiki/Christian_cross "Principal symbol of Christianity")
infobox.value: | * [Jesus](/wiki/Jesus_in_Christianity "Jesus in Christianity") * [Christ](/wiki/Christ_(title) "Christ (title)")  * [Nativity](/wiki/Nativity_of_Jesus "Nativity of Jesus") * [Baptism](/wiki/Baptism_of_Jesus "Baptism of Jesus") * [Ministry](/wiki/Ministry_of_Jesus "Ministry of Jesus") * [Crucifixion](/wiki/Crucifixion_of_Jesus "Crucifixion of Jesus") * [Resurrection](/wiki/Resurrection_of_Jesus "Resurrection of Jesus") * [Ascension](/wiki/Ascension_of_Jesus "Ascension of Jesus")

is an [ordained](/wiki/Ordained "Ordained") member of the [clergy](/wiki/Clergy "Clergy") who is entrusted with a position of [authority and oversight](/wiki/Episcopal_polity "Episcopal polity") in a religious institution. In [Christianity](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity"), bishops are normally responsible for the governance and administration of [dioceses](/wiki/Diocese "Diocese"). The role or office of the bishop is called **episcopacy** or the **episcopate**. Organisationally, several [Christian denominations](/wiki/Christian_denomination "Christian denomination") utilise ecclesiastical structures that call for the position of bishops, while other denominations have dispensed with this office, seeing it as a symbol of power. Bishops have also exercised political authority within their dioceses.

Leadership position in religious institutions

A **bishop** is an [ordained](/wiki/Ordained "Ordained") member of the [clergy](/wiki/Clergy "Clergy") who is entrusted with a position of [authority and oversight](/wiki/Episcopal_polity "Episcopal polity") in a religious institution. In [Christianity](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity"), bishops are normally responsible for the governance and administration of [dioceses](/wiki/Diocese "Diocese"). The role or office of the bishop is called **episcopacy** or the **episcopate**. Organisationally, several [Christian denominations](/wiki/Christian_denomination "Christian denomination") utilise ecclesiastical structures that call for the position of bishops, while other denominations have dispensed with this office, seeing it as a symbol of power. Bishops have also exercised political authority within their dioceses.

Traditionally, bishops claim [apostolic succession](/wiki/Apostolic_succession "Apostolic succession") and the [historic episcopacy](/wiki/Historical_episcopate "Historical episcopate"), a direct historical lineage dating back to the original [Twelve Apostles](/wiki/Twelve_Apostles "Twelve Apostles") or [Saint Paul](/wiki/Saint_Paul "Saint Paul"). The bishops are by doctrine understood as those who possess the full [priesthood](/wiki/Priest#Christianity "Priest") given by [Jesus Christ](/wiki/Jesus_in_Christianity "Jesus in Christianity"), and therefore may ordain other clergy, including other bishops.[[1]](#cite_note-1) A person ordained as a [deacon](/wiki/Deacon "Deacon"), priest (i.e. [presbyter](/wiki/Presbyter "Presbyter")), and then bishop is understood to hold the fullness of the [ministerial](/wiki/Minister_(Christianity) "Minister (Christianity)") priesthood, given responsibility by Christ to govern, teach and [sanctify](/wiki/Sanctify "Sanctify") the [Body of Christ](/wiki/Body_of_Christ "Body of Christ") (the [Christian Church](/wiki/Christian_Church "Christian Church")). Priests, deacons and [lay ministers](/wiki/Laity "Laity") co-operate and assist their bishops in [pastoral](/wiki/Pastor "Pastor") ministry.

Some [Pentecostal](/wiki/Pentecostal "Pentecostal") and other [Protestant](/wiki/Protestant "Protestant") denominations have bishops who oversee congregations, though they do not necessarily claim apostolic succession, with exception to those Pentecostals and Charismatics affiliated to churches founded by [J. Delano Ellis](/wiki/J._Delano_Ellis "J. Delano Ellis") and [Paul S. Morton](/wiki/Paul_S._Morton "Paul S. Morton").

## Etymology and terminology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bishop&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Etymology and terminology")]

The English word *bishop* derives, via [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin") *episcopus*, [Old English](/wiki/Old_English "Old English") *biscop*, and [Middle English](/wiki/Middle_English "Middle English") *bisshop*, from the [Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greek "Ancient Greek") word ἐπίσκοπος, *epískopos*, meaning "overseer" or "supervisor".[[2]](#cite_note-BIA-2) Greek was the language of the early Christian church,[[3]](#cite_note-ccel_Earl-3) but the term *epískopos* did not originate in Christianity: it had been used in Greek for several centuries before the advent of Christianity.[[2]](#cite_note-BIA-2)

The English words *priest* and *presbyter* both derive, via Latin, from the Greek word πρεσβύτερος, *presbýteros*, meaning "elder" or "senior", and not originally referring to priesthood.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

In the early Christian era the two terms were not always clearly distinguished, but *epískopos* is used in the sense of the order or office of bishop, distinct from that of *presbýteros*, in the writings attributed to [Ignatius of Antioch](/wiki/Ignatius_of_Antioch "Ignatius of Antioch") in the second century.[[3]](#cite_note-ccel_Earl-3)

## Christian episcopal development

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bishop&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Christian episcopal development")]

The earliest organization of the [Church in Jerusalem](/wiki/Early_centers_of_Christianity#Jerusalem "Early centers of Christianity") was, according to most scholars, similar to that of Jewish [synagogues](/wiki/Synagogue "Synagogue"), but it had a council or college of ordained [presbyters](/wiki/Presbyters "Presbyters") (πρεσβύτεροι, *elders*). In Acts 11:30[[5]](#cite_note-5) and Acts 15:22,[[6]](#cite_note-6) a collegiate system of government in Jerusalem is chaired by [James the Just](/wiki/James,_brother_of_Jesus "James, brother of Jesus"), according to tradition the [first bishop of the city](/wiki/Greek_Orthodox_Patriarch_of_Jerusalem#Jewish_Bishops_of_Jerusalem "Greek Orthodox Patriarch of Jerusalem"). In Acts 14:23,[[7]](#cite_note-7) the [Apostle Paul](/wiki/Paul_of_Tarsus "Paul of Tarsus") ordains presbyters in [churches in Anatolia](/wiki/Early_centers_of_Christianity#Anatolia "Early centers of Christianity").[[8]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHill2007-8) The word *presbyter* was not yet distinguished from *overseer* (ἐπίσκοπος, *episkopos*, later used exclusively to mean *bishop*), as in Acts 20:17,[[9]](#cite_note-9) Titus 1:5–7[[10]](#cite_note-10) and 1 Peter 5:1.[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[a]](#cite_note-13)[[b]](#cite_note-15) The earliest writings of the [Apostolic Fathers](/wiki/Apostolic_Fathers "Apostolic Fathers"), the [Didache](/wiki/Didache "Didache") and the [First Epistle of Clement](/wiki/First_Epistle_of_Clement "First Epistle of Clement"), for example, show the church used two terms for local church offices—presbyters (seen by many as an interchangeable term with *episkopos* or overseer) and deacon.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/44/Augustine_Lateran.jpg/250px-Augustine_Lateran.jpg)](/wiki/File:Augustine_Lateran.jpg)

A 6th-century image of [Saint Augustine](/wiki/Augustine_of_Hippo "Augustine of Hippo"), bishop of [Hippo Regius](/wiki/Hippo_Regius "Hippo Regius")[[14]](#cite_note-WDL-16)

In the [First Epistle to Timothy](/wiki/First_Epistle_to_Timothy "First Epistle to Timothy") and [Epistle to Titus](/wiki/Epistle_to_Titus "Epistle to Titus") in the New Testament a more clearly defined episcopate can be seen. Both letters state that Paul had left Timothy in Ephesus and Titus in [Crete](/wiki/Early_centers_of_Christianity#Greece "Early centers of Christianity") to oversee the [local church](/wiki/Church_(congregation) "Church (congregation)").[[15]](#cite_note-17)[[16]](#cite_note-18) Paul commands Titus to ordain presbyters/bishops and to exercise general oversight. John Zizioulas argues that "The task of the Bishop was from the beginning principally liturgical, consisting in the offering of the Divine Eucharist."[[17]](#cite_note-19) The [authorship](/wiki/Authorship_of_the_Pauline_epistles "Authorship of the Pauline epistles") of both those letters is questioned by many scholars in the field and the question whether they reflect a first or second century structure of church hierarchy is among the arguments used in the debate as to their authenticity.

Early sources are unclear but various groups of Christian communities may have had the bishop surrounded by a group or college functioning as leaders of the local churches.[[18]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEO'Grady1997140-20)[[19]](#cite_note-21) Eventually the head or "monarchic" bishop came to rule more clearly,[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVan_Hove1907-22) and all local churches would eventually follow the example of the other churches and structure themselves after the model of the others with the one bishop in clearer charge,[[18]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEO'Grady1997140-20) though the role of the body of presbyters remained important.[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVan_Hove1907-22)

### Apostolic Fathers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bishop&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Apostolic Fathers")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/18/Byzantine_-_Saint_Ignatius_of_Antioch_-_Walters_4820867.jpg/250px-Byzantine_-_Saint_Ignatius_of_Antioch_-_Walters_4820867.jpg)](/wiki/File:Byzantine_-_Saint_Ignatius_of_Antioch_-_Walters_4820867.jpg)

Ignatius, bishop of Antioch, student of [John the Apostle](/wiki/John_the_Apostle "John the Apostle")

Around the end of the [1st century](/wiki/Christianity_in_the_1st_century "Christianity in the 1st century"), the early church's organization became clearer in historical documents.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] In the works of the Apostolic Fathers, and Ignatius of Antioch in particular, the role of the episkopos, or bishop, became more important or, rather, already was very important and being clearly defined. Other contemporary Christian writers do not describe monarchial bishops, either continuing to equate them with the presbyters or speaking of *episkopoi* (bishops, plural) in a city.

[Clement of Alexandria](/wiki/Clement_of_Alexandria "Clement of Alexandria") (end of the 2nd century) writes about the ordination of a certain Zachæus as bishop by the imposition of [Simon Peter Bar-Jonah's](/wiki/Saint_Peter "Saint Peter") hands. The words bishop and ordination are used in their technical meaning by the same Clement of Alexandria.[[21]](#cite_note-23) The bishops in the 2nd century are defined also as the only clergy to whom the ordination to priesthood ([presbyterate](/wiki/Presbyterate "Presbyterate")) and diaconate is entrusted: "a priest (presbyter) [lays on hands](/wiki/Laying_on_of_hands "Laying on of hands"), but does not [ordain](/wiki/Ordain "Ordain")." (*cheirothetei ou cheirotonei*).[[22]](#cite_note-24)

At the beginning of the 3rd century, [Hippolytus of Rome](/wiki/Hippolytus_of_Rome "Hippolytus of Rome") describes another feature of the ministry of a bishop, which is that of the *"Spiritum primatus sacerdotii habere potestatem dimittere peccata"*: the primate of sacrificial priesthood and the power to forgive sins.[[23]](#cite_note-web._Whyt-25)

### Canonical age

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bishop&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Canonical age")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/52/Patriarch_Stephen_I%2C_Madrid_Skylitzes_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Patriarch_Stephen_I%2C_Madrid_Skylitzes_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Patriarch_Stephen_I,_Madrid_Skylitzes_(cropped).jpg)

Patriarch Stephen I of Constantinople was ordained as bishop at the age of 19

As the bishop's role further developed into the 4th century, the [First Council of Nicaea](/wiki/First_Council_of_Nicaea "First Council of Nicaea") decreed that bishops should be ordained by at least three others.[[24]](#cite_note-26) Age requirements for episcopal ordination or consecration were neither universal nor fixed in early Christian churches.[[25]](#cite_note-www.newadvent.org-27) It was, however, universally required that a bishop be male.

Lacking a definitive ecumenical age requirement for holy orders—between the early [ecumenical councils](/wiki/Ecumenical_council "Ecumenical council") of the [Great](/wiki/Great_Church "Great Church") and imperial Roman churches, and after schism into the Latin and Greek churches—young men had been ordained, appointed, and/or enthroned as bishops, some as young as 5.[[26]](#cite_note-28)

Notable younger Latin and Greek bishops have included: [Hugh Vermandois](/wiki/Hugh_of_Vermandois_(bishop) "Hugh of Vermandois (bishop)") (5); [Luis Antonio Jaime de Borbón y Farnesio](/wiki/Luis_Antonio_Jaime_de_Borb%C3%B3n_y_Farnesio "Luis Antonio Jaime de Borbón y Farnesio") (8); [Guido Ascanio Sforza di Santa Fiora](/wiki/Guido_Ascanio_Sforza_di_Santa_Fiora "Guido Ascanio Sforza di Santa Fiora") (9); [Benedict IX](/wiki/Pope_Benedict_IX "Pope Benedict IX") (11-20); [Karol Ferdynand Vasa](/wiki/Karol_Ferdynand_Vasa "Karol Ferdynand Vasa") (11); [Alexander Stewart](/wiki/Alexander_Stewart_(archbishop_of_St_Andrews) "Alexander Stewart (archbishop of St Andrews)") (11); [Niccolò Caetani](/wiki/Niccol%C3%B2_Caetani "Niccolò Caetani") (13); [Bruno von Bayern](/wiki/Bruno_of_Augsburg "Bruno of Augsburg") (14); Delano Ellis](/wiki/J._Delano_Ellis "J. Delano Ellis") (born 1944), co-founder of the [Joint College of African-American Pentecostal Bishops](/wiki/Joint_College_of_African-American_Pentecostal_Bishops "Joint College of African-American Pentecostal Bishops"), was also elevated as bishop at the age of 26 in 1970.[[39]](#cite_note-41)[[40]](#cite_note-42)

## Christian bishops and civil government

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bishop&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Christian bishops and civil government")]

See also: [State church of the Roman Empire](/wiki/State_church_of_the_Roman_Empire "State church of the Roman Empire")

The efficient organization of the [Roman Empire](/wiki/Roman_Empire "Roman Empire") became the template for the organisation of the [Great Church](/wiki/Great_Church "Great Church") in the [4th century](/wiki/Christianity_in_the_4th_century "Christianity in the 4th century"), particularly after [Constantine's](/wiki/Constantine_I_and_Christianity "Constantine I and Christianity") [Edict of Milan](/wiki/Edict_of_Milan "Edict of Milan"). As the church moved from the shadows of privacy into the public forum it acquired land for churches, burials and [clergy](/wiki/Clergy "Clergy"). In 391, [Theodosius I](/wiki/Theodosius_I "Theodosius I") decreed that any land that had been confiscated from the church by Roman authorities be returned.[[41]](#cite_note-43)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fe/Husaby_Church_2013_people_on_11th_Century_gravestone.jpg/250px-Husaby_Church_2013_people_on_11th_Century_gravestone.jpg)](/wiki/File:Husaby_Church_2013_people_on_11th_Century_gravestone.jpg)

A bishop with other officials on an 11th-century grave in [Sweden](/wiki/Sweden "Sweden")

The most usual term for the geographic area of a bishop's authority and ministry, the [diocese](/wiki/Diocese "Diocese"), began as part of the structure of the Roman Empire under [Diocletian](/wiki/Diocletian "Diocletian"). As [Roman authority began to fail in the western portion of the empire](/wiki/Fall_of_the_Western_Roman_Empire "Fall of the Western Roman Empire"), the church took over much of the civil administration.[[42]](#cite_note-44) This can be clearly seen in the ministry of two [popes](/wiki/Pope "Pope"): [Pope Leo I](/wiki/Pope_Leo_I "Pope Leo I") in the [5th century](/wiki/Christianity_in_the_5th_century "Christianity in the 5th century"), and [Pope Gregory I](/wiki/Pope_Gregory_I "Pope Gregory I") in the [6th century](/wiki/Christianity_in_the_6th_century "Christianity in the 6th century"). Both of these men were statesmen and public administrators in addition to their role as Christian pastors, teachers and leaders. In the [Eastern churches](/wiki/Eastern_Roman_Empire "Eastern Roman Empire"), [latifundia](/wiki/Latifundia "Latifundia") entailed to a bishop's [see](/wiki/Episcopal_see "Episcopal see") were much less common, the state power did not collapse the way it did in the West, and thus the tendency of bishops acquiring civil power was much weaker than in the West. However, the role of Western bishops as civil authorities, often called [prince bishops](/wiki/Prince-Bishop "Prince-Bishop"), continued throughout much of the [Middle Ages](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages").[[43]](#cite_note-45)

### Bishops holding political office

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bishop&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Bishops holding political office")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3f/Winstrup%2C_Peder_%28in_Pandectae%2C_1666%29.jpg/250px-Winstrup%2C_Peder_%28in_Pandectae%2C_1666%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Winstrup,_Peder_(in_Pandectae,_1666).jpg)

[Peder Winstrup](/wiki/Peder_Winstrup "Peder Winstrup") a Danish bishop

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/9/9c/Johann_Otto_von_Gemmingen.jpg)](/wiki/File:Johann_Otto_von_Gemmingen.jpg)

[Johann Otto von Gemmingen](/wiki/Johann_Otto_von_Gemmingen "Johann Otto von Gemmingen"), [Prince-Bishop of Augsburg](/wiki/Prince-Bishopric_of_Augsburg "Prince-Bishopric of Augsburg")

As well as being [archchancellors](/wiki/Archchancellor "Archchancellor") of the [Holy Roman Empire](/wiki/Holy_Roman_Empire "Holy Roman Empire") after the 9th century, bishops generally served as [chancellors](/wiki/Chancellor "Chancellor") to medieval monarchs, acting as head of the *justiciary* and chief [chaplain](/wiki/Chaplain "Chaplain"). The [Lord Chancellor](/wiki/Lord_Chancellor "Lord Chancellor") of [England](/wiki/England "England") was almost always a bishop up until the dismissal of [Cardinal](/wiki/Cardinal_(Catholic_Church) "Cardinal (Catholic Church)") [Thomas Wolsey](/wiki/Thomas_Wolsey "Thomas Wolsey") by [Henry VIII](/wiki/Henry_VIII_of_England "Henry VIII of England").[[44]](#cite_note-46) Similarly, the position of [Kanclerz](/wiki/Kanclerz "Kanclerz") in the [Polish kingdom](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Poland_(1385%E2%80%931569) "Kingdom of Poland (1385–1569)") was always held by a bishop until the [16th century](/wiki/Christianity_in_the_16th_century "Christianity in the 16th century").[[45]](#cite_note-47)

In modern times, the principality of [Andorra](/wiki/Andorra "Andorra") is headed by [Co-Princes of Andorra](/wiki/Co-Princes_of_Andorra "Co-Princes of Andorra"), one of whom is the [Bishop of Urgell](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Diocese_of_Urgell "Roman Catholic Diocese of Urgell") and the other, the sitting [President of France](/wiki/President_of_France "President of France"), an arrangement that began with the [Paréage of Andorra (1278)](/wiki/Par%C3%A9age_of_Andorra_(1278) "Paréage of Andorra (1278)"), and was ratified in the 1993 constitution of Andorra.[[46]](#cite_note-unesco-48)

The [office of the Papacy](/wiki/Pope "Pope") is inherently held by the sitting Roman Catholic Bishop of Rome.[[47]](#cite_note-49)[[48]](#cite_note-50) Though not originally intended to hold temporal authority, since the Middle Ages the power of the Roman papacy gradually expanded deep into the secular realm and for centuries the sitting Bishop of Rome was the most powerful governmental office in Central Italy.[[49]](#cite_note-Faus-51) In modern times, the Pope of Rome is also the sovereign Prince of [Vatican City](/wiki/Vatican_City "Vatican City"), an internationally recognized [micro-state](/wiki/Micro-state "Micro-state") located entirely within the city of Rome.[[50]](#cite_note-52)[[51]](#cite_note-53)[[52]](#cite_note-54)[[53]](#cite_note-Geopolitics-55)

In [France](/wiki/France "France"), prior to [the Revolution](/wiki/French_Revolution "French Revolution"), representatives of the clergy—in practice, bishops and [abbots](/wiki/Abbot "Abbot") of the largest [monasteries](/wiki/Monastery "Monastery")—comprised the [First Estate](/wiki/Estates_of_the_realm#First_Estate "Estates of the realm") of the [Estates-General](/wiki/French_States-General "French States-General"). This role was abolished after separation of church and state was implemented during the French Revolution.[[54]](#cite_note-56)

In the 21st century, the more senior bishops of the [Church of England](/wiki/Church_of_England "Church of England") continue to sit in the [House of Lords](/wiki/House_of_Lords "House of Lords") of the [Parliament of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Parliament_of_the_United_Kingdom "Parliament of the United Kingdom"), as representatives of the [established church](/wiki/Established_church "Established church"), and are known as [Lords Spiritual](/wiki/Lords_Spiritual "Lords Spiritual"). The [Bishop of Sodor and Man](/wiki/Bishop_of_Sodor_and_Man "Bishop of Sodor and Man"), whose diocese lies outside the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom"), is an [*ex officio* member](/wiki/Ex_officio_member "Ex officio member") of the [Legislative Council of the Isle of Man](/wiki/Legislative_Council_of_the_Isle_of_Man "Legislative Council of the Isle of Man").[[55]](#cite_note-57) In the past, the [Bishop of Durham](/wiki/Bishop_of_Durham "Bishop of Durham") had extensive vice-regal powers within his northern diocese, which was a [county palatine](/wiki/County_palatine "County palatine"), the [County Palatine of Durham](/wiki/County_Palatine_of_Durham "County Palatine of Durham"), (previously, [Liberty of Durham](/wiki/Liberty_of_Durham "Liberty of Durham")) of which he was *ex officio* the [earl](/wiki/Earl "Earl"). In the 19th century, a gradual process of reform was enacted, with the majority of the bishop's historic powers vested in [The Crown](/wiki/The_Crown "The Crown") by 1858.[[56]](#cite_note-durham1858-58)

[Eastern Orthodox](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox_Church "Eastern Orthodox Church") bishops, along with all other members of the clergy, are [canonically](/wiki/Canon_law "Canon law") forbidden to hold political office.[[57]](#cite_note-59) Occasional exceptions to this rule are tolerated when the alternative is political chaos. In the [Ottoman Empire](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire"), the [Patriarch of Constantinople](/wiki/Ecumenical_Patriarch_of_Constantinople "Ecumenical Patriarch of Constantinople"), for example, had de facto administrative, cultural and legal jurisdiction,[[58]](#cite_note-60) as well as spiritual authority, over all Eastern Orthodox Christians of the empire, as part of the Ottoman [millet](/wiki/Millet_(Ottoman_Empire) "Millet (Ottoman Empire)") system. An Eastern Orthodox bishop headed the [Prince-Bishopric of Montenegro](/wiki/Prince-Bishopric_of_Montenegro "Prince-Bishopric of Montenegro") from 1516 to 1852, assisted by a secular *[guvernadur](/wiki/Guvernadur "Guvernadur")*. More recently, Archbishop [Makarios III](/wiki/Makarios_III "Makarios III") of [Cyprus](/wiki/Cyprus "Cyprus"), served as [President](/wiki/President_of_Cyprus "President of Cyprus") of the [Cyprus](/wiki/Cyprus "Cyprus") from 1960 to 1977, an extremely turbulent time period on the island.[[59]](#cite_note-61)

In 2001, [Peter Hollingworth](/wiki/Peter_Hollingworth "Peter Hollingworth"), [AC](/wiki/List_of_Companions_of_the_Order_of_Australia "List of Companions of the Order of Australia"), [OBE](/wiki/Order_of_the_British_Empire "Order of the British Empire")—then the Anglican Archbishop of [Brisbane](/wiki/Anglican_Diocese_of_Brisbane "Anglican Diocese of Brisbane")—was controversially appointed [Governor-General of Australia](/wiki/Governor-General_of_Australia "Governor-General of Australia"). Although Hollingworth gave up his episcopal position to accept the appointment, it still attracted considerable opposition in a country which maintains a formal [separation between Church and State](/wiki/Separation_of_church_and_state "Separation of church and state").[[60]](#cite_note-62)[[61]](#cite_note-63)

### Episcopacy during the English Civil War

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bishop&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Episcopacy during the English Civil War")]

Main article: [Episcopacy during the English Civil War](/wiki/Episcopacy_during_the_English_Civil_War "Episcopacy during the English Civil War")

During the period of the [English Civil War](/wiki/English_Civil_War "English Civil War"), the role of bishops as wielders of political power and as upholders of the established church became a matter of heated political controversy. [Presbyterianism](/wiki/Presbyterianism "Presbyterianism") was the polity of most [Reformed Christianity](/wiki/Reformed_Christianity "Reformed Christianity") in Europe, and had been favored by many in England since the English Reformation. Since in the [primitive Church](/wiki/Apostolic_Age "Apostolic Age") the offices of *presbyter* and *episkopos* were not clearly distinguished, many [Puritans](/wiki/Puritans "Puritans") held that this was the only form of government the church should have. The Anglican divine, [Richard Hooker](/wiki/Richard_Hooker "Richard Hooker"), objected to this claim in his famous work *Of the Laws of Ecclesiastic Polity* while, at the same time, defending Presbyterian ordination as valid (in particular [Calvin's](/wiki/John_Calvin "John Calvin") ordination of [Beza](/wiki/Theodore_Beza "Theodore Beza")). This was the official stance of the English Church until the Commonwealth, during which time, the views of Presbyterians and Independents ([Congregationalists](/wiki/Congregationalists "Congregationalists")) were more freely expressed and practiced.

## Christian churches

### Catholic, Eastern and Oriental Orthodox, Lutheran and Anglican churches

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bishop&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Catholic, Eastern and Oriental Orthodox, Lutheran and Anglican churches")]

Further information: [Bishops in the Catholic Church](/wiki/Bishops_in_the_Catholic_Church "Bishops in the Catholic Church")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f9/Mitre_%28plain%29.svg/120px-Mitre_%28plain%29.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Mitre_(plain).svg)

A [mitre](/wiki/Mitre "Mitre") is used as a symbol of the bishop's ministry in Western Christianity.
